senses.definition: An organic compound that is an azine formed from an aldehyde, with the general chemical formula RCH=NN=CHR.
examples: The chemist synthesized an aldazine from a simple aromatic aldehyde to test its properties.
Aldazine ligands can act as color-changing sensors for certain metal ions in solution.
Students analyzed the structure of an aldazine in their advanced organic chemistry class.

senses.definition: A small village, especially in a rural Spanish-speaking area.
examples: They spent the summer in a quiet aldea in northern Spain.
The aldea has only one shop and a small church in the center.
Many young people leave the aldea to look for work in the city.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or obsolete word, probably intended to mean the action of building or forming a village, but not used in modern English.
examples: The term aldeament does not appear in contemporary dictionaries.
Researchers found the word aldeament only in a few historical documents.

senses.definition: The plural form of aldea, meaning small villages in rural Spanish-speaking areas.
examples: The region is dotted with tiny aldeas connected by narrow roads.
Electricity only reached some of the remote aldeas in recent decades.
Tourists visit the aldeas to experience traditional village life.

senses.definition: A bright orange-red star in the constellation Taurus, seen as the eye of the bull and one of the brightest stars in the night sky.
examples: On clear winter nights, you can easily see Aldebaran near the Orion constellation.
Aldebaran marks the eye of Taurus in many star maps.
Astronomers measured the distance from Earth to Aldebaran using modern instruments.

senses.definition: A fictional or obsolete name that has been used in science fiction or old texts for an imaginary material or element connected with the star Aldebaran.
examples: The spaceship was powered by crystals of aldebaranium in the novel.
Early pulp magazines sometimes mentioned exotic metals like aldebaranium.

senses.definition: An old chemical term used for certain alcohols related to aldehydes, no longer common in modern chemistry.
examples: The name aldehol appears in some nineteenth-century chemistry books.
Modern chemists rarely use the term aldehol, preferring systematic names instead.

senses.definition: An enzyme that helps break down or change aldehydes in living organisms.
examples: Aldehydase enzymes play an important role in detoxifying harmful aldehydes in the body.
Researchers studied aldehydase activity in liver tissue samples.

senses.definition: A chemical substance formed from an alcohol, with a special carbonyl group, used in many industrial and biological processes.
examples: Formaldehyde is a simple aldehyde used in making plastics and resins.
Many aldehyde compounds have strong, sharp smells.
In organic chemistry, students learn how to oxidize alcohols to aldehydes.

senses.definition: The plural of aldehyde, referring to a group of organic compounds that contain an aldehyde functional group.
examples: Aromatic aldehydes are widely used in fragrances and flavorings.
The laboratory keeps different aldehydes in well-sealed bottles because of their strong odors.
Aldehydes and ketones are studied together in many chemistry courses.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the qualities of an aldehyde, especially its chemical group or sharp smell.
examples: The perfume has a bright, aldehydic top note that makes it feel sparkling.
They studied the aldehydic nature of the compound in the laboratory.
Some wines develop aldehydic aromas when exposed to too much air.

senses.definition: An old or rare name for certain compounds related to aldehydes, no longer used in standard chemical naming.
examples: The term aldehydine appears in some historical chemistry articles.
Modern textbooks do not usually mention aldehydine as a separate class.

senses.definition: A historical chemical term for a substance formed when an aldehyde combines with water or another molecule, not used in modern naming.
examples: Researchers found the word aldehydrol in early twentieth-century journals.
Aldehydrol is not a term you will see in recent chemistry literature.

senses.definition: A small village or community, especially in Portuguese-speaking countries such as Brazil or Portugal.
examples: They visited a fishing aldeia on the Atlantic coast of Portugal.
The aldeia depends on agriculture and tourism for income.
Many traditions in the aldeia have been passed down for generations.

senses.definition: A first name or family name used in English-speaking countries, for both people and places.
examples: Alden grew up in a small town before moving to the city.
The famous educator Henry Alden wrote several books.
They spent their holiday near Alden Lake in Minnesota.

senses.definition: A type of tree that often grows in wet ground, with small cones and soft wood.
examples: The riverbank was lined with alder trees and willows.
They used alder for the wooden parts of the instrument.
Alder leaves began to fall as autumn arrived.

senses.definition: An old name for the star Alpha Cephei, a bright star in the constellation Cepheus.
examples: Early star charts refer to Alpha Cephei as Alderamin.
Alderamin is one of the main stars used to outline the shape of Cepheus.
Astronomers noted that Alderamin will move closer to the north celestial pole over time.

senses.definition: Insects with long wings that live near water and belong to the same group as dobsonflies, often used as models in fishing.
examples: Alderflies lay their eggs on plants hanging over the river.
Anglers sometimes tie artificial alderflies to catch trout.
The pond was full of alderflies in late spring.

senses.definition: A brown or dark insect with soft wings that lives near freshwater and whose larvae live in water.
examples: The alderfly rested on a reed above the stream.
Biology students identified the insect as an alderfly.
The presence of alderfly larvae can show that the water is quite clean.

senses.definition: An old poetic word meaning 'dearest' or 'most beloved'.
examples: She was his alderliefest friend and confidante in every matter.
In the poem, the knight calls the lady his alderliefest one.
To my alderliefest child, I leave all my books, the letter began.

senses.definition: A very rare or obsolete word, possibly meaning a young alder tree or something related to alder, not used in modern English.
examples: The term alderling appears only in a few historical glossaries.
Most speakers of English today would not recognize the word alderling.

senses.definition: A member of a city or town council, especially in some English-speaking countries, who helps make local laws and decisions.
examples: The alderman spoke against the proposed tax increase.
She served as an alderman for eight years before becoming mayor.
Residents wrote letters to their alderman about the new parking rules.

senses.definition: The position, office, or term of service of an alderman.
examples: He looked back on his aldermanate as a time of major change in the city.
Her aldermanate lasted for three full terms.
The reforms were introduced during his aldermanate in the 1990s.

senses.definition: A very rare humorous or invented word, apparently combining 'alderman' and 'necromancy', used jokingly about local politics.
examples: The newspaper joked that only aldermancy could explain the council's strange decision.
Talk of aldermancy spread after the mysterious budget changes.

senses.definition: An old or rare word for a female alderman, or the wife of an alderman.
examples: The aldermaness attended many official receptions at the town hall.
Some nineteenth-century records refer to her as an aldermaness.

senses.definition: Relating to an alderman or the office of alderman.
examples: The aldermanic election drew a low voter turnout.
She took on several aldermanic responsibilities after the resignation.
The city expanded the aldermanic districts to reflect population growth.

senses.definition: Having the quality or style of an alderman, especially in a formal or somewhat old-fashioned way.
examples: He wore an aldermanical chain of office during the ceremony.
The hall was decorated in an aldermanical style with portraits of former council leaders.

senses.definition: A playful or extremely rare word that might mean the actions, habits, or features typical of aldermen.
examples: The satirist mocked the aldermanities of the local council members.
His diary is full of notes about the daily aldermanities of town government.

senses.definition: A very rare or humorous abstract noun based on alderman, referring to the state or quality of being an alderman.
examples: The novel gently laughs at the pomp and aldermanity of small-town politics.
He spoke with great aldermanity, as if the whole city depended on his words.

senses.definition: Behaving in a way thought suitable for an alderman, often suggesting seriousness, dignity, or a love of rich food and comfort.
examples: He gave an aldermanlike speech praising the city's long history.
The character is a cheerful, aldermanlike figure who enjoys big meals.
Her aldermanlike manner impressed the older members of the council.

senses.definition: In a manner typical of an alderman, especially suggesting formality, respectability, or a well-fed appearance.
examples: He walked with an aldermanly air through the council chamber.
Her aldermanly voice carried clearly across the room.
The story describes an aldermanly gentleman with a large waistcoat.

senses.definition: The plural of aldermanry, meaning different districts or offices held by aldermen.
examples: Several aldermanries were merged to create a larger ward.
Historical records list the names of all aldermanries in the city.
Reforms reduced the number of aldermanries from twelve to eight.

senses.definition: The office, district, or dignity of an alderman.
examples: He accepted the aldermanry after many years of community service.
Her aldermanry covered one of the oldest parts of the city.
The council voted to abolish the traditional aldermanry system.

senses.definition: The position of being an alderman or the skills and qualities expected of an alderman.
examples: He was praised for his aldermanship during difficult budget negotiations.
Her aldermanship combined firmness with a sense of fairness.
The party rewarded his years of aldermanship with a higher office.

senses.definition: The plural of aldermanship, referring to several periods of service or positions as alderman.
examples: His long career included three aldermanships in different wards.
The report lists all aldermanships held by members over the last century.

senses.definition: The plural of alderman, meaning members of a city or town council in some English-speaking countries.
examples: The aldermen voted to approve the new housing plan.
Several aldermen opposed the project because of its cost.
Citizens met with the aldermen to express their concerns.

senses.definition: An archaic form of 'alder', relating to alder trees.
examples: The poet wrote of aldern woods beside the river.
Old texts sometimes mention aldern groves in the countryside.

senses.definition: A small island in the English Channel, part of the Channel Islands and under British Crown dependency.
examples: They took a ferry from Guernsey to Alderney for the weekend.
Alderney is known for its quiet beaches and wildlife.
The history of Alderney includes periods of military importance.

senses.definition: The plural of alder, referring to several alder trees.
examples: A line of alders marked the edge of the wetland.
Beavers had gnawed many of the alders along the stream.
The forest there is mainly birches and alders.

senses.definition: A female member of a city or town council, especially in some English-speaking countries.
examples: The alderwoman introduced a new plan for public transport.
Citizens praised the alderwoman for her work on housing issues.
She became the first alderwoman in the town's history.

senses.definition: The plural of alderwoman, referring to female members of a city or town council.
examples: Several alderwomen spoke in favor of the education reforms.
The council now includes more alderwomen than ever before.
Citizens met with aldermen and alderwomen to discuss the plan.

senses.definition: An old or alternative name used in astronomy for a particular star in the constellation Leo, now rarely used.
examples: Some star catalogues list the star under the traditional name Aldhafara.
The guide mentioned Aldhafara as one of several historic star names.
Modern astronomers prefer standardized designations instead of names like Aldhafara.

senses.definition: A traditional star name used in astronomy, probably a variant spelling of Aldhafara, for a star in the constellation Leo.
examples: Older charts sometimes use the name Aldhafera for the same star.
The lecturer explained that Aldhafera and similar names come from Arabic.
Aldhafera is rarely used in modern scientific catalogues.

senses.definition: A very toxic chemical used as a pesticide to kill insects and worms that damage crops.
examples: Because aldicarb is highly poisonous, its use is tightly controlled.
The farmer stopped using aldicarb after stricter safety rules were introduced.
Scientists studied the effects of aldicarb on groundwater quality.

senses.definition: Different forms or products of the pesticide aldicarb.
examples: Regulations limit the sale of aldicarbs in many countries.
Scientists compared several aldicarbs for their impact on soil organisms.

senses.definition: A rare chemistry term for a compound related to an aldehyde, sometimes used in naming reactions or classes of substances.
examples: The reaction produces an aldide as an intermediate product.
Older papers refer to certain aldehyde derivatives as aldides.

senses.definition: A shortened or variant form used in chemistry for an aldimine, a compound formed from an aldehyde and ammonia or an amine.
examples: The mechanism passes through an aldim intermediate.
Formation of the aldim is a key step in the reaction pathway.

senses.definition: An older name for an aldimine, a type of imine formed from an aldehyde and ammonia or an amine.
examples: The textbook from the early 1900s uses the term aldime instead of aldimine.
The reaction between the aldehyde and the amine gives an aldime.

senses.definition: A rare variant of the word aldimine, meaning an imine formed from an aldehyde and an amine.
examples: The compound was first described as an aldimin in German literature.
The synthesis proceeds via an aldimin intermediate.

senses.definition: An imine formed when an aldehyde reacts with ammonia or an amine, containing a carbon–nitrogen double bond.
examples: The reaction between the aldehyde and the amine forms an aldimine.
Aldimine intermediates are important in many biochemical processes.
Chemists monitored the formation of the aldimine by spectroscopy.
